title: Bike Touring Vlog - Semijarvi, Finland to Amsterdam, Netherlands 8 & 9-2010 1/5

transcript.text: Hello, this is Travel Blog Bike Tour, from Simi Harvey, Finland, all the way to the end
Of my bike tour.
I already did this video while I was tripping on mushrooms, on the Hawaiian stuff that I
Got from Tatanka.nl in Amsterdam, the strongest of the shit.
The only problem is, it mysteriously vanished off of my card and my, well, I took it off
The card, off of my computer, which is a bummer, because I didn't even get to see it.
I wanted to see what I looked like tripping, talking about my travels.
It was basically the same as being stoned.
Basically the same.
It's like being really stoned.
But anyways, the last one I did was in Simi Harvey, Finland.
Okay, so, yeah, Simi Harvey, Finland I had ridden, 370 miles, and over 33 hours, including
Three hours, where I passed out in that burger place.
And then I stayed there two days because I had, because it was raining really hard, and
I wanted to get caught up on some, like, internet stuff, like videos, you know?
And, um, yeah.
Plus my butt was really hurting, like I did that 240 mile ride from Helsinki to Kuopio,
And my, and after a week and a half of not riding my bike, and like my left butt muscle,
My gluteus maximus, was like, we were just feeling like if I hammer really hard, like,
Or not if I hammer hard, if I ride hard, reasonably hard, it would like spasm.
Um, your butt muscle spasming feels really weird.
It's really painful, but it feels, like, good.
I don't know.
Anyways.
But so then I, so yeah, so then I left Simi Harvey at like 1245 or something, 1145, 1245.
I think it was 1245.
And I rode all day, and, um, it was really beautiful.
Um, and I had to get water, and like, the lady, the girl at some place, like at the edge
Of some lake, and it wasn't so far after this town, I bought water, and I had to pay like
Six euros just for like a bottle of like water, because she wouldn't let me fill the water up,
Because they said they had, it was only for them or something.
It's freaking expensive up there.
So then I rode all day, and then at midnight, it was still light out.
It's so awesome to ride when the sun never goes down.
Like, it's so amazing.
I have to do it again.
Um, and I went and I got a bunch of food at some burger place right before it closed.
And then I started riding again, and I rode all night, and it was beautiful riding up
Through, um, lakes and stuff, you know.
And I was seeing like fishermen fishing at like, you know, one, two, three, four in the
Morning.
So yeah, when it's light out, you just stay up whenever you want.
It's pretty cool.
Um, and then I, I was going to ride all day the next day.
I was going to try and like ride 400 miles, you know, break my 300 mile, 370 mile record.
It's like a nice round number.
So it's definitely one of my goals to ride 400 miles in one ride, not in under 24 hours.
Unless I'm in a race, that would be cool.
But, um, but like my left butt cheek start was like really hurting me.
Um, and I wanted the experience of sleeping all day and then riding all night.
So I got to the border of Norway and I actually crossed into Norway at like seven in the morning
When I got there after 266 miles.
And, um, I slept during the day and it was really weird.
It was really cool though.
Wasn't too hot because it was really far up north.
So it was about perfect.
I just laid on top of my sleeping bag, you know, let the ventilation go through and it
Was nice.
I had a nice slip.
I got up at about seven at night or something like that, eight and rode across the river
Again into Finland, that town, which I can't remember, had a pizza.
They had internet.
Um, and I did the internet thing, you know, answering YouTube, YouTube comments basically
And, um, that kind of thing.
And, um, and then I got some food and then I rode the night started at like 10 30 or something.
And I rode and I was going to go to, um, uh, Vardo just cause I wanted to go like that
Far tip of Northern Norway.
And then I want to go all the way up to the Northern tip of Norway, the, uh, what's it
Called?
Um, Nord cap, but about 10 kilometers or something before I got to Vadsau and Vadsau is about 85
Miles from, um, that place, that border town.
Um, my chain started slipping and I thought it was because my chain was bad, but then
I realized after that it was actually my big chain ring that was bad.
So I roll into town and I go to some hotel and I was just curious how much they charge.
It's not like I was going to stay there, but it was 120 euros a night.
And the kid there was like, Oh, here, if you pay a little bit of money, I think, I don't
Know, I can't remember how much it was.
I could have a shower and that's what I wanted and a, uh, and I could soak in the hot sauna.
So I just laid, had a shower late in the sauna until like noon or whatever.
And then I came out and then I had their brunch, their buffet for 20 bucks.
It's all you can eat the most amazing buffet I've ever had in my life, like caviar, like
Everything.
And I was eating it so, so long that I finally felt like guilty about eating more, even though
I should have eaten more because I was still hungry and doing the dinner at the same time.
And then I step out and a car drives by and lo and behold, it's my friend.
Um, um, oh wait, Jesus, I'm drawing a blank.
Um, Norwegian name.
Oh shit.
Yeah.
Who I was hanging out with on the origins in my, one of the guys in my YouTube video from
Greece, um, partying with in Greece and Athens.
He's like, Hey Kyle, what's up?
So, cause he's from there.
So then I rode like 10 kilometers north of town and hung out with him at his job for like
An hour.
And then, uh, I went back and, uh, to town and, um, I know, and then camped that day
Right on the beach.
But I put my tent on the beach, like right, right on the edge of the beach, like where I'm
Like, Oh, look, sand, really soft.
I mean, really smooth.
So I put my tent there and, uh, next thing I know, like my tent collapses and I'm like,
Why would my tent collapse?
I, I, I, I put a huge rock on top of that stake, you know, that it keeps the tire tight.
So I get out and the fucking ocean had melted my tent and I'm about to be swallowed by the
Ocean because of the rising tide.
And I look up and there's this old Norwegian guy with his wife there going, ha ha, it's
A siglo.
I was like, and there was, it was a really beautiful beach.
It was like mainland.
And then it's this little, actually in English, they're called Kyle.
That's the English word for it.
They have the Kyle and the really thin strip of land.
And then you have the peninsula.
So I was on the Kyle and, um, um, and there was like houses laying the whole thing.
It was really pretty place right next to the, um, um, museum where my friend worked.
So the next day I go into town and I, and I, um, I had to, um, I was like getting food or
Just wandering around a small little town.
Um, um, and then I got the chain ring was like more than a hundred bucks just for the
Chain ring at some bike shop, but at least they had it.
And then I kind of had dinner and then I decided to do another night ride.
So I left at like eight 30 and I rode all night long from VADSO to, um, carry shock.
One of the two, I think it's the capital of, um, Lapland of Norway.
I can't remember what it's called.
The capital of a Sammy village, Sammy town, the natives.

They didn't look like Sammies to me.
Although I guess a lot of Sammies look totally white, but, um, and it was still a kind of
The place and it was like, yeah, I rode all night and it was cool.
Never getting dark, but by now my butt, like I was starting to go downhill.
Um, my butt was like really, um, sore.
Um, and I couldn't ride hard at all.
I had to ride really slowly and I can't remember.
I think it was like 160 miles and to, uh, carry shock.
And my, I'd been doing the chat with my brother in the library in the, in VADSO the day before.
And he's like, dude, Norway's so expensive.
I have to get the fuck hell out of here.
When are you going to be able to meet me in Trondheim?
Cause we were going to meet up in Trondheim.
And I was like, all right, I'll just take the bus down there and skip, um, skip, uh,
Nordcap, the northernmost tip of Norway.
So he's like, okay, good.
Thanks.
I'll see you there in a couple of days.
I'm like, yeah, all right, I'll let you know.
So, um, I could have ridden my bike from Karaschok through Kartikano, which is the other Sámi town,
Down, um, into, uh, Sweden to the Swedish railway and taking the rail system from Sweden all around into, um, Trondheim.
But I would have had to ride that day, like another couple hundred miles or something, two, three hundred miles or something pretty far.
And, um, I just didn't, you know, I wanted to, um, I didn't want to risk not being able to, you know, meet my brother in time and, you know, and just, and suffer for that to meet him there in time.
And so I was just like, all right, fuck it.
I'll just take the bus there.
And, but I wanted to, but I'll take the bus.
Um, but the best way to take the bus was to go way up north.
And I wanted to see up north in the fjords anyway.
So it worked out.
So I took the bus all the way up to the northern tip of, before the peninsula goes up to, um, Nordcap to some town.
I can't remember what it's called.
And, um, it was a pretty beautiful town, you know, right on the northern tip there of the continent.
And I get on, I go to a movie theater.
I want to see a movie.

And he get the bike tourist kid was on some, like, he was kind of at the beginning of his trip.
He wanted to ride.
He was an English kid from like England all the way up to Nordcap down to the north, southern tip of, uh, Africa up to the, you know, where the Bering Strait, you know, part of Russia.
And then down to the northern, to like Singapore, you know, the four corners of the world.
Uh, and it was supposed to be a four-year trip and he was sponsored by Lifeboat Tee and his, his, his, uh, website was charliesomething.com.
But I can't remember.
But anyway, he was hanging out there with his computers, like, looking at cool, like, YouTube videos or whatever with his guys he befriended at the movie.
And then I went in to go watch a movie and the guy's like, ah, you go for free.
So that was cool because movies are like 15 bucks.
And, um, and, um, then I camped right in the town, like right, like one block from where I would get on the bus.
It was really cool behind some building where I had, uh, dinner.
And it was light all night, but I slept well.
Got on the bus and then rode the bus.
And I have the YouTube video of it going through the, um, the edge of Norway, the, the fjords.
Probably the most beautiful area I've ever been in my entire life.
Um, I mean, misty, mist everywhere, foggy, misty, um, um, reindeer everywhere.
Reindeer are deer.
They're the size of Colorado deer.
You know, not nearly as big as elk.
Oh, shit, I just have a whole bunch of blood coming out of my leg.
I accidentally broke a scab or something.
I love the taste of blood.
Um, except they have horns.
They have horns the size of elk horns, huge horns, and they're furry horns.
So you're like, how the hell do they get around?
Because, I mean, because I've seen them.
Like when I was going up through, uh, Finland, I saw a whole bunch of those.
And like what they do is they look, stand in the middle of the road and just stare at you as you come close.
And once you get to about maybe a hundred meters from them or a little less, they'd run into the bushes.
But some, but their horns are so big that they had, when they run into the trees, you know,
Forest, they have to be really careful.
They have to like sneak and kind of duck their head in and do like that.
But, um, yeah, I saw a whole bunch of those up there.
But that's the only animal I saw in Finland, man.
I didn't see anything.
Well, I saw a couple of rats running, running across, scammering, scammering across.
But I saw no roadkill anywhere in Scandinavia, like once, which was awesome because in like
Romania, oh my God, like every five minutes I'd see roadkill and they'd all have collars
On them, cats, dogs.
I was like, what the hell is this?
Do these Romanians have too many animals or do they, are they trying to kill them?
I don't know what the deal was.
But yeah, but no roadkill, no animals either.
And also, yeah, though, I mean, there were like roadkill in Australia with the, um, yeah,
The, uh, um, the kangaroo and everywhere, every five seconds you'd see one.
I saw thousands of them, never saw a live kangaroo.

But no dead ones.
